title: South Korea’s Biggest Criminal Justice Reform
gist: South Korea is an outlier among developed democracies for centralizing investigation and prosecution power in one prosecutorial body, a structure the author likens to autocracies, which has allowed prosecutors to wield outsized political influence and sets up the country's biggest criminal justice reform.

themes.theme: The separation-of-powers norm South Korea breaks from

insights.claim: France's 1808 legal reform established the cornerstone of modern prosecution by separating investigation, prosecution, and trial among different parties, a separation-of-powers model that most of Europe and today's developed liberal democracies have since followed.
insights.speaker: Eunwoo Lee
insights.so_what: The 1808 French model is the global benchmark for prosecutorial checks and balances against which South Korea's system is being judged as an outlier.
insights.locator: France's legal reform in 1808 laid down the cornerstone

insights.claim: Combining investigation and prosecution powers within a single body has almost inevitably led to judicial prejudice and abuse of power historically, which is why most developed democracies separated these functions.
insights.speaker: Eunwoo Lee
insights.so_what: This causal claim is the analytical basis for why South Korea's concentrated prosecutorial model is viewed as structurally prone to abuse, framing the case for reform.
insights.locator: Putting any of the two elements within one hand

themes.theme: South Korea's outlier, autocracy-style prosecutorial monopoly

insights.claim: South Korea's prosecution system is highly centralized with a monopoly on both investigation and prosecution, a structural model the author says is more commonly found in autocracies than developed democracies.
insights.speaker: Eunwoo Lee
insights.so_what: South Korea's concentrated prosecutorial power is a structural governance outlier among developed democracies, a red flag for institutional/rule-of-law risk assessments of the country.
insights.locator: South Korea, however, is an exception

insights.claim: South Korean prosecutors also exercise coercive powers normally reserved for police in other countries, and they have used these expansive legal capabilities to influence South Korean politics behind the scenes.
insights.speaker: Eunwoo Lee
insights.so_what: Prosecutorial overreach into politics is a governance risk factor for South Korea that investors should weigh when assessing political and regulatory predictability, and it is the direct rationale for the 'biggest criminal justice reform' referenced in the headline.
insights.locator: The prosecutors also exercise coercive powers
